text: Hello, I'm sharing a cholecystectomy in a gentleman who developed a calculus cholecystitis while recovering from open heart surgery a couple of months ago, was temporized with a percutaneous cholecystostomy tube.
They have been optimized. They are off their anticoagulation for the procedure and they present for cholecystectomy.
The surgeon removes the percutaneous cholecystostomy tube, allowing for retraction of the gallbladder above the liver.
The surgeon clears fatty tissue from the bottom of the gallbladder, exposing the area where the cystic duct and artery are presumed to be located.
The surgeon observes a significant amount of fatty tissue covering the base of the gallbladder and the area of the presumed Calot's triangle.
The surgeon begins dissection by removing fatty tissue to access the lateral aspect of the gallbladder wall, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon continues dissection along the lateral aspect of the gallbladder, progressively clearing fatty tissue to expose underlying structures.
The surgeon observes the artery positioned extremely anteriorly relative to the gallbladder during the dissection.
The surgeon grasps the fatty tissue and moves it to the medial side to facilitate opening the peritoneum medially.
The surgeon adopts a top-down approach to increase the mobility of the gallbladder, aiming to facilitate the dissection around the cystic duct and artery.
The surgeon continues dissection down the gallbladder, encountering a posterior artery during the process.
The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to gain control over the tissue, likely in the area where the artery was noted to be extremely anterior.

The surgeon returns to the lower area of the surgical field, attempting to identify the cystic duct emerging from a green structure believed to be the common duct. The surgeon continues thinning the surrounding fat to expose the gallbladder wall, with the green structure appearing to be the cystic duct.
The surgeon encircles the artery, attempting to further mobilize it while ensuring it is the cystic artery and not extending back into the liver.

The surgeon observes the gallbladder after completing a top-down dissection from the liver, noting the absence of additional arterial structures from the anterior medial aspect of the gallbladder into the liver, confirming the presence of a very anteriorly based cystic artery.
The surgeon identifies the gallbladder wall and the cystic duct, focusing on safely thinning out the surrounding tissue to improve visibility and access.
The surgeon prepares to apply clips to secure the cystic duct, using a bipolar instrument for control.
The surgeon carefully approaches the gallbladder wall, aiming to thin out the surrounding tissue for better visualization and access.
The surgeon identifies the bifurcation of the cystic duct branching off from the common duct, clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies a clearer dissection plane at the bottom of the gallbladder, allowing for more confident and precise dissection in this safe anatomical location.
The surgeon identifies the cystic duct and proceeds to thin out the surrounding tissue to safely isolate the duct from adjacent structures.
Which is what I'm doing here.

The surgeon completes the thinning of the cystic duct by dissecting posteriorly, carefully pulling it away from the common duct. The anatomical structures are clearly visible, allowing for safe dissection in this area.
The surgeon identifies the common bile duct, visible in the surgical field, adjacent to the bifurcation of the cystic duct.
The gallbladder is visualized funneling down to the cystic duct. The surgeon continues to thin out the tissue in this area to prepare for the placement of surgical clips.
The surgeon clips the artery and duct emerging from the porta hepatis, then proceeds to cut both structures.
The surgeon completes the cholecystectomy and inspects the surgical site, ensuring meticulous hemostasis before the patient is restarted on anticoagulation therapy.
But overall pleased with how the procedure went and hope you enjoyed.
